lens.included_activities: HMO-owned medical centers with physicians and other medical staff providing recurring primary or specialized outpatient care to HMO subscribers, where the integrated HMO-provider enterprise or a separable licensed care-delivery platform is a transferable going concern in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.excluded_activities: Independent physician, dental, or other practitioner groups merely contracting with prepaid plans; carrier-only underwriting and administration; hospitals; nonseparable captive locations that cannot be acquired as a control business; shells; and operations outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Subscribers receive outpatient care through an HMO-owned delivery system funded from premiums, capitation, internal medical budgets, and sometimes service-level reimbursements. Revenue is recurring with enrollment, while care delivery is governed by network, quality, access, and medical-loss-ratio economics.

a.bridge: Source population: BLS employment for broad NAICS 621400 Outpatient Care Centers and O*NET task descriptions for registered nurses, medical assistants, and medical secretaries, rather than 621491 wage-weighted tasks. Quantity transformation: weight the clearly digital scheduling, billing, record, correspondence, intake, documentation, and decision-support portions of those roles while reserving examination, treatment, medication administration, physical procedures, and accountable clinical judgment. Error direction: broad outpatient data may overstate exposure if HMO centers employ more physicians or understate it if integrated HMOs centralize unusually large administrative and population-health functions.
a.rationale: Outpatient centers have a substantial administrative layer, and clinical roles themselves include records, care planning, intake, routing, and patient communication that can be assisted or partly substituted. The majority of clinical delivery remains embodied, licensed, and judgment-heavy, limiting the exposed share.
a.change_evidence: A 621491-specific payroll extract by occupation linked to time-and-motion data for scheduling, coding, inbox, charting, prior-authorization, care-gap outreach, and face-to-face clinical tasks would materially move the range.
caveats: BLS publishes the occupational mix at broad NAICS 621400, which also includes dialysis, surgery, emergency, and other outpatient centers.
O*NET describes occupations nationally and does not measure current automation or wage-weighted task time inside HMO medical centers.

rho.bridge: Source population: national certified-health-IT rules and occupational workflows, not implementations at lower-middle-market HMO medical centers. Quantity transformation: treat standardized electronic health information and available predictive decision support as infrastructure for workflow automation, then discount exposed tasks for integration, validation, privacy, clinician oversight, labor redesign, and patient-safety controls. Error direction: this may overstate realization at small legacy centers or understate it where an integrated HMO can deploy one platform across plan and provider operations.
rho.rationale: Integrated plan-provider data can support automated intake, chart summarization, coding assistance, scheduling, care-gap outreach, message triage, and clinical decision support. Five-year implementation is constrained by fragmented systems, false positives, workflow liability, privacy, model monitoring, clinician acceptance, and the need to retain licensed human decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Audited pre/post implementations from HMO-owned centers reporting labor hours per visit, denial and coding error rates, safety events, override rates, and sustained staffing changes by workflow would materially move rho.
caveats: HTI-1 establishes health-IT transparency and interoperability conditions, not measured labor realization.
No 621491-specific adoption, error-rate, or staffing study was found.

e.rationale: Census defines these centers as owned by the HMO and also includes establishments that combine care delivery with insurance underwriting. That makes many establishments captive components rather than standalone outsourced-service targets. Eligibility is concentrated in a small subset where the entire HMO-provider enterprise fits the band or the care platform, contracts, licenses, staff, and subscriber relationships can transfer together.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 621491 ownership, insurance licensure, legal entities, EBITDA, separability, change-of-control requirements, and related-party revenue would materially move e.
caveats: The frozen n estimate uses provider-margin bridging even though Census includes integrated insurer-provider establishments.
No national measure of separable, control-acquirable HMO medical-center firms in the target EBITDA band was found.

s5.bridge: Source population: Gallup's national survey of employer-business owners across industries, where 22% planned to sell or transfer within five years. Quantity transformation: reduce that intention benchmark for the institutional or captive ownership typical of HMO medical centers, regulated insurance change-of-control, the very small eligible pool, and the gap between plans and completed qualifying sales. Error direction: it may understate strategic divestitures of provider assets or overstate completed control transfers because intentions include family transfers and failed processes.
s5.rationale: A qualifying transfer is less likely than the cross-industry owner-intention benchmark because many centers are not owner-operated standalone firms and acquisitions can require provider, payer, and insurance approvals. Strategic sales or combinations remain possible, producing a nonzero but low five-year range.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from state insurance change-of-control filings, HMO license records, provider M&A databases, and interviews with HMO boards or owners would materially move s5.
caveats: Gallup intentions are not industry-specific and include transfers that may not be arm's-length control sales.
The tiny estimated firm population makes realized availability highly discrete.

q.rationale: An integrated HMO can initially retain lower clinic operating cost within premium or capitation revenue, and better access or coding can add value. Retention decays through premium competition, rate review, benefit expansion, provider compensation, reinvestment, and medical-loss-ratio requirements; Medicare Advantage plans face an 85% minimum MLR and sanctions or remittance below it.
q.change_evidence: Plan- and center-level contracts plus five years of medical-cost, MLR, premium, rebate, provider-compensation, and renewal data before and after automation would materially move q.
caveats: The 85% MLR rule directly covers Medicare Advantage and related CMS plan contracts, not every commercial or Medicaid HMO arrangement.
No evidence directly measures the durable share of clinic automation benefits retained by target-band HMO owners.

d5.bridge: Source population: BLS projects broad NAICS 621400 real output to grow 3.5% annually from 2024 to 2034, while CMS expected Medicare Advantage enrollment to be broadly stable in 2026 after plan projections of 34.0 million versus 34.9 million in 2025. Quantity transformation: translate the broad outpatient outlook to a five-year direction, then discount sharply for the much narrower HMO-owned-center format, stable near-term MA enrollment, payer exits, and shifts between owned and contracted networks. Error direction: the estimate may be high if HMOs move care to contracted practices or low if integrated primary care gains share in growing managed-care populations.
d5.rationale: Outpatient care demand should remain supported by population aging, chronic-disease management, and a broad real-output growth outlook, but 621491 depends on the organizational choice to own centers rather than merely contract with clinicians. Stable Medicare Advantage enrollment is supportive but not a direct HMO-center volume forecast.
d5.change_evidence: A 621491 revenue and visit series in constant prices, split by commercial, Medicaid, and Medicare HMO enrollment and by owned versus contracted delivery, would materially move d5.
caveats: NAICS 621400 includes several faster- or slower-growing outpatient models unrelated to HMO medical centers.
Medicare Advantage is only one HMO market and enrollment does not map one-for-one to visits at owned centers.

o.rationale: Most primary and specialized outpatient care still requires a licensed, accountable provider organization for examination, diagnosis, treatment, prescribing, quality, and escalation. Software and virtual self-service can eliminate administrative encounters and some low-acuity visits, while HMOs can bypass owned centers by contracting with physician groups that Census classifies elsewhere, so operator survival for this exact lens is lower than clinical-care survival overall.
o.change_evidence: HMO claims and encounter data separating owned-center, contracted-practice, virtual-only, and self-service episodes over time, plus plan network-strategy interviews, would materially move o.
caveats: No observed five-year rate of owned-center bypass or virtual substitution was found.
The estimate distinguishes continued need for medical care from continued need for an HMO-owned medical-center operator.

narrative.executive_view: HMO medical centers offer meaningful administrative and clinical-support automation, but the industry definition itself creates a severe acquisition-eligibility problem: the centers are HMO-owned and may be inseparable from regulated insurance operations. The operating thesis is therefore narrower than a conventional physician-practice roll-up and depends on finding a transferable integrated or carve-out platform.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can reduce work in scheduling, intake, chart preparation, coding, claims support, message routing, care-gap outreach, documentation, referral coordination, and population-health prioritization. Examination, diagnosis, prescribing, treatment, safeguarding, and consequential patient decisions remain licensed human responsibilities with audited decision support.
narrative.value_capture: Integrated premium or capitation economics can preserve near-term cost savings, but competitive premiums, medical-loss-ratio rules, benefit commitments, provider compensation, compliance, and technology reinvestment pass much of the gain back to subscribers, purchasers, clinicians, or regulators over time.
narrative.firm_availability: Availability is the central constraint. Census says these establishments are owned by the HMO, so many are captive assets rather than independent targets; qualifying opportunities require an entire small HMO-provider enterprise or a genuinely separable care-delivery platform with transferable contracts, licenses, staff, and subscriber access.
narrative.demand_durability: Outpatient primary and chronic care are durable, and broad outpatient real output is projected to grow. Demand for this exact format is less certain because HMOs may use contracted physician networks or virtual channels instead of owned centers, and current Medicare Advantage enrollment evidence is stable rather than strongly growing.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major risks are misclassification of captive assets as eligible firms, insurance and provider change-of-control approvals, medical-loss-ratio pass-through, payer concentration, adverse selection, coding and risk-adjustment scrutiny, model bias or unsafe recommendations, privacy and cybersecurity, clinician resistance, and shifts from owned centers to contracted networks.
narrative.principal_driver: The principal driver is the combination of a high labor share with integrated plan-provider data that can automate administrative work and augment repeat outpatient workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: The principal weakness is structural eligibility: NAICS 621491 centers are HMO-owned, so very few may be standalone transferable businesses under the default roll-up lens.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau NAICS Sector 62 definitions: 621491 HMO Medical Centers
sources.supports: Industry boundary, HMO ownership, included integrated underwriting-provider establishments, and cross-reference to independently contracting practitioners.

sources.title: BLS May 2023 OEWS: NAICS 621400 Outpatient Care Centers
sources.supports: Broad outpatient occupational mix, including 15.71% office and administrative support, 15.39% registered nurses, 6.63% medical assistants, and 4.70% medical secretaries.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Registered Nurses
sources.supports: Clinical assessment, care planning, medical-record, medication, education, and case-management tasks used to separate digital support from embodied licensed care.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Medical Assistants
sources.supports: Administrative and clinical task mix including scheduling, records, billing, coding, intake, vital signs, specimens, and medication support.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Medical Secretaries and Administrative Assistants
sources.supports: Scheduling, billing, chart, report, correspondence, message-routing, and medical-record workflows used for task exposure.

sources.title: ASTP/ONC HTI-1 Final Rule overview
sources.supports: Certified-health-IT interoperability and algorithm-transparency requirements, including fairness, appropriateness, validity, effectiveness, and safety assessment.

sources.title: CMS: Medicare Advantage and Medicare Prescription Drug Programs Expected to Remain Stable in 2026
sources.supports: CMS projection of 34.0 million Medicare Advantage enrollees in 2026 versus 34.9 million in 2025 and its expectation of broadly stable enrollment.

sources.title: CMS Medical Loss Ratio
sources.supports: Medicare Advantage minimum 85% medical-loss-ratio requirement and sanctions or remittance for noncompliance, used for eligibility and retention constraints.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Cross-industry benchmark that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years.

sources.title: BLS Employment Projections: Employment and output by industry
sources.supports: Broad NAICS 621400 real-output projection from 217.8 to 307.4 billion chained 2017 dollars, a 3.5% annual rate for 2024-2034.
